summary: Studies jailbreak defense for a single narrowly-scoped forbidden behavior (bomb-making help). Safety training, adversarial training, and input/output classifiers all fall short; a proposed transcript-classifier defense beats these baselines but still fails in some cases, showing narrow-domain defense remains hard.

Introduces a sandbagging detector that injects noise of varying magnitude into model weights: honest models degrade predictably while sandbagging models show anomalous performance gains. The signal is model-agnostic and even elicits Mistral Large 120B's full capability when it underperforms unprompted.

Extends dictionary learning to model diffing: an SAE dictionary trained on a pre-finetuning transformer is itself finetuned on the new data or model, so tracking feature evolution isolates dataset- versus model-driven changes. Applied to sleeper agents, it surfaces backdoor-related features.
Announces the Anthropic Fellows Program, a pilot that funds and mentors external researchers to work on AI safety, aiming to accelerate safety research and grow talent in the field.

A framework for fast, high-quality 3D human avatar generation and personalization from text prompts. By constraining the generation search space, it improves photo-realism and efficiency over prior text-to-avatar methods.

An addendum to the AlphaFold 3 paper. AlphaFold 3 is a diffusion-based model that jointly predicts the 3D structure of biomolecular complexes-proteins, nucleic acids, small molecules, ions, and modified residues-markedly improving accuracy over earlier specialized tools. The addendum supplements the original article.

Tests the 3D gamma index as an extra check for safely transferring radiotherapy plans between nominally beam-matched linacs. Using PyMedPhys on 17 3DCRT and 36 VMAT plans, 2%/2 mm pass rates exceeded 99%, though stricter 1%/1 mm criteria failed in several cases.

Tests whether LLMs truly perform latent multi-hop reasoning over factual knowledge rather than exploiting training-data shortcuts. Introduces SOCRATES, a shortcut-controlled evaluation set built to exclude co-occurring entity pairs, and measures how well models internally recall and compose separately-learned facts.

Investigates using LLM embeddings of string inputs as features for regression. Embedding-based features can beat traditional feature engineering on high-dimensional tasks, partly because embeddings preserve Lipschitz continuity over numeric inputs; larger model size and language understanding do not reliably improve regression accuracy.

Introduces RE-Bench, seven open-ended ML research-engineering environments with data from 71 8-hour expert attempts as a human baseline. Top AI agents score 4x humans at a 2-hour budget, but humans overtake with more time; agents iterate far faster and cheaper.

Outlines OpenAI's approach to combining human and automated red teaming, accompanied by two papers: one detailing how external human red-teaming campaigns are designed and run, and one on automatically generating diverse, effective attacks using reinforcement learning.

Uses sparse autoencoders to find that LLMs represent entity recognition-whether the model can recall facts about an entity-as causal directions in activation space. Steering these directions induces refusal on known entities or hallucination on unknown ones; chat finetuning repurposes this base-model mechanism.

Proposes a Neural Radiance Field representation for non-opaque scenes that bakes quadrature fields into textured polygons, enabling fast inference while preserving NeRF's high-quality novel-view rendering and avoiding costly dense volume sampling.

Introduces UniIR, an instruction-guided universal multimodal retriever that handles heterogeneous retrieval tasks (e.g., text-to-image, image-to-text) in a single model, plus the M-BEIR benchmark for training and evaluating retrieval across diverse modality combinations.

Develops a recurrent transformer neural-network decoder for the surface code that outperforms state-of-the-art decoders on Google's Sycamore data at distances 3 and 5, scales to distance 11 in simulation, and adapts to real hardware noise from limited experimental samples.

Reports InterPro v101, which classifies over 200 million protein sequences into 85,000 families and domains by integrating member-database signatures. Updates include deeper AlphaFold structure integration and AI-assisted family descriptions, with over 5,000 new entries added in two years.

Introduces ViSTa, 4,000+ videos with step-by-step descriptions across virtual-home, Minecraft, and real-world settings, hierarchically structured to test vision-language models on sequential tasks. State-of-the-art VLMs recognize objects well but largely fail to judge task sequences, with only GPT-4o showing non-trivial performance.

Argues for reporting model-eval results with statistical rigor and gives concrete recommendations: use the Central Limit Theorem for standard errors, cluster errors for related questions, reduce variance, and run paired analyses comparing models on the same questions to detect real capability differences.

Presents Value Imprint, a framework that audits the human values embedded in RLHF datasets. Using a values taxonomy and a trained classifier on Anthropic HH-RLHF, OpenAI WebGPT, and Alpaca-GPT4 data, it finds information/knowledge values dominate while prosocial and democratic values are underrepresented.

Proposes a conceptual framework unifying the many forms of human feedback used to train RL agents. Defines a feedback taxonomy across nine dimensions and seven quality metrics, then derives design requirements for systems that learn from human feedback, bridging ML and HCI.

Updates the Pfam protein-families database: decommissioned the standalone site and merged into InterPro, harmonized with the ECOD structural classification, and expanded metagenomic and microprotein curation. AlphaFold structures refine domain boundaries, and the deep-learning Pfam-N expands UniProtKB coverage by 8.8%.

Argues that democratizing AI requires democratic governance and alignment, especially for systemically impactful decisions. Proposes a Democracy Levels framework with milestones and tools to guide organizations and evaluate efforts like Collective Constitutional AI toward more legitimate, participatory AI decision-making.

Presents SIMPL, an EM-style algorithm that jointly and scalably optimizes latent variables and tuning curves from neural activity, using behavior as initialization. On a large hippocampal dataset it yields smaller, more numerous place fields, suggesting finer spatial encoding than behavior-based analyses imply.

Studies soundness notions for interactive oracle proofs (IOPs), a proof-system model generalizing interactive proofs and PCPs that underpins many succinct arguments. Establishes new results clarifying the relationships among soundness definitions for IOPs.

Proposes blocking whole classes of jailbreaks rapidly after seeing only a few examples, rather than seeking perfect robustness. Introduces RapidResponseBench; the best method finetunes an input classifier on automatically proliferated jailbreaks, cutting attack success over 240x in-distribution and over 15x out-of-distribution from one example per strategy.

Examines AI red teaming through a social-and-collaborative-computing lens, surfacing under-studied human factors: how red teamers are selected, their biases and blind spots, and the psychological toll of harmful content. Sets a workshop agenda connecting red teaming to data-labeling and content-moderation research.

Proposes that humans achieve compositional generalization by abstracting and recombining knowledge of subprocess dynamics. Across two sequence-learning studies, participants transferred learned subprocess knowledge to novel composite tasks; predictive-representation models lacking transfer failed to fit the behavior.

Builds Flamingo-CXR, a chest-radiograph report generator, and evaluates it with board-certified radiologists. Preferences varied widely: AI reports matched or beat clinicians in 56% of ICU and 78% of in/outpatient cases; both report types contained errors, motivating a clinician-AI collaborative composition setting.

Argues intervention is a core goal of interpretability and unifies sparse autoencoders, logit lens, tuned lens, and probing into one encoder-decoder framework. New metrics (intervention success rate, coherence-intervention tradeoff) show control is possible but inconsistent, with lens methods outperforming SAEs and probes on simple concepts.

Introduces SimpleQA, a benchmark of short, fact-seeking questions with single indisputable answers, adversarially collected against GPT-4 and easy to grade as correct, incorrect, or not-attempted. Designed to measure whether models know what they know, rewarding accurate answers and appropriate abstention.
